segments.s075-g25: ز ... تا marks a range: 'from X to Y.' Here ز (preposition) begins the range, برج بره names zodiacal points (برج = zodiac sign, بره = the Ram/Aries), and ترازو (literally 'scale' = the Balance/Libra) closes the range; جهان (the world) is the topic referenced by that range. Word order is poetic (prepositional range before the topical noun).
segments.s075-g26: همی is an adverb of continuation ('still/always') modifying the clause; تیرگی is the subject/noun 'darkness.' دارد is the present-tense lexical verb 'has/holds' used here like 'lies' or 'dwells'; اندر نهان is a locative phrase 'within, hidden.' The order places the adverb/verb close to the noun in typical archaic/poetic style.
segments.s075-g27: چنین 'thus' introduces the statement; تا ... شود is a clause of limit/result meaning 'until ... becomes.' ز گردش is an ablative/causal 'by/through the rotation' (here astronomical rotation), به ماهی شود = 'becomes/takes place in the (sign) Fish (Pisces).' شود is the present/poetic passive/relational 'becomes.'
segments.s075-g28: پر از is the adjectival phrase 'full of'; تیرگی و سیاهی are coordinated nouns 'darkness and blackness/gloom'; شود again is 'becomes' — the clause states the resulting condition. Note the adjective-like use of پر with از to mean 'filled with.'
segments.s075-g29: دو سرو‌ای is a poetic nomination ('two cypresses' with the vocative/poetic -ای flavor); the following phrase is appositive: دو بازوی چرخ بلند = 'two tall/lofty arms of the sky/heaven' (چرخ = heaven/sky). Adjective بلند follows the noun, a common Persian order.
segments.s075-g30: کزو is a relative/ablative 'from which' linking back to the cypresses; نیمه ... و نیمی ... is parallel balance 'one half ... and the other half ...'; شادب describes one half as 'cheerful/graceful' and نژند labels the other half 'base/worthless.' The construction divides the thing into two contrasting halves.
segments.s075-g31: برو is an imperative 'go' or 'fly off,' addressed to مرغ پران ('winged bird' — vocative). چو + noun + دان here functions as a command 'consider/regard (it) as the sun' (چو = 'as/like'); دان is used imperatively/verbally 'consider' (poetic/archaic usage).
segments.s075-g32: جهان را uses را to mark the definite object/topic 'the world'; ازو is ablative 'from it' (referring back to the sun); بیم و امید is the coordinated pair 'fear and hope.' دان here is 'know'/'be aware' (declarative imperative), so the clause states: know that the world's fear and hope are from it. Object marker placement (جهان را) fronted for emphasis is typical in Persian.
segments.s075-g41: This clause is SOV-style: همه (an emphatic quantifier) + رنج ما (the noun 'suffering' with the possessive pronoun 'our') + ماند (past tense 'remained' / 'was left'). زی خارستان is a following locative/prepositional phrase: زی is an archaic preposition meaning 'in/from' and خارستان 'thorn‑thicket' names the place where the suffering stayed.
segments.s075-g42: گذر کرد is a verbal phrase built from the noun گذر ('passage') + the light verb کرد ('to do/make'), giving 'to pass / to make a passage'. باید is a modal 'it is necessary / must'; in Classical Persian the modal can follow the verbal phrase as here. سوی شارستان is the directional prepositional phrase 'toward the town'.
segments.s075-g43: کسی دیگر is 'no one else / another person' (here indefinite/negative sense); از رنج ما is a prepositional phrase 'from/with our suffering' where رنج ما is the noun + possessive; برخورد is a noun/verb meaning 'encounter / meeting' used here as the event that should not happen to anyone else.
segments.s075-g44: نپاید is a negative jussive/subjunctive meaning 'should not befall / should not happen' (classical usage). برو is the imperative 'go' addressed to someone; نیز و هم are adverbs 'also / likewise' reinforcing the instruction; بگذرد is a subjunctive/optative 'let it pass' or 'may it pass' (third person). Together: an injunction to move on so that the pain may pass.
segments.s075-g45: چنین is an adverb 'thus / so' modifying رفت, the past tense verb 'went / proceeded' describing how the tale ran. از آغاز 'from the beginning' is a temporal/prepositional phrase, and یکسر سخن ('the whole / entire speech/story') is the object/phrase being described — یکسر functions adverbially/quantitatively to mean 'entirely'.
segments.s075-g46: همین باشد is an optative/jussive formula 'let this be (so) / this is enough' where باشد is the subjunctive/jussive form of 'to be'. و links the clause to نو نگردد کهن: نو 'new' + نگردد (negative present/subjunctive of گشتن/گردیدن) 'may not become' + کهن 'old/ancient' — i.e. 'and may the new not turn old.'
segments.s075-g47: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). توشه‌مان is a noun with the first‑person plural possessive suffix 'our provision / store'. نیکنامی is a predicate noun 'good name / reputation'. بود is the past/inferential copula 'were / would be' marking the counterfactual or hypothetical condition.
segments.s075-g48: روانها is the plural subject 'the souls'. بران is the contracted prepositional/locative form 'thereon / thereby' (on that account). سر here participates in the predicative phrase 'سر گرامی بود' — literally 'the head was honored' but idiomatically 'be held in honor / be esteemed'; گرامی is 'honored / dear', and بود is the copula 'would be'. The whole means 'the souls would thereby be honored.'
segments.s075-g33: دگر precedes the noun and here means “another/other”; شارستان is the noun “city/town.” The preposition بر + سر کوهسار places the town spatially “on/at the summit of the mountain,” with سر functioning as “summit/top.” Poetic word order omits the ezāfe and places modifiers directly.
segments.s075-g34: سرای here is a noun “dwelling/abode” (not in the provided sense list, so marked unknown); درنگست is an archaic/perfective-present copular form meaning “is a place of lingering / it lingers” (verbal predicate describing the سرای). The coordinating و links the two nominal predicates, giving جای قرار “a place of rest/settlement” as a second characterization of the dwelling.
segments.s075-g35: همین is an emphatic demonstrative “this very” placed before خارستان “thorn-thicket/wasteland.” چون introduces a simile “like”; سرای (here ‘house/abode’) combines with سپنج “sponge” as a metaphorical compound (“a sponge-like house”)—again the poetic lexicon omits an explicit connector (ezāfe) but reads as ‘this thorn-bush is like a sponge-house.’
segments.s075-g36: کزو is a relative/compound prepositional phrase “from which / from it.” The coordination links two pairs: ناز و گنجست — “(there) are/are found) nâz (vanity/caressing) and treasure” with گنجست functioning as a verbal copula/existential “is/are treasure-contained.” The following و هم درد و رنج adds “and also pain and hardship,” with هم emphasizing addition.
segments.s075-g37: همی is a habitual/continuous particle “continually, keeps on.” دم زدن is an idiom built from noun + زدن meaning “to take a breath/utter/make a move”; the duplicated preposition بر introduces the object تو before the main verb بشمرد (“he counts/holds against”). The result is a habitual present action counted as blame or reckoning toward the addressee.
segments.s075-g38: The correlative structure هم او ... هم او ... places two actions in parallel: او is third-person subject; برفرازد is a classical-present verb meaning “he raises/lifts up;” بشکرد is an archaic verb meaning “he breaks/destroys.” The repetition with هم emphasizes that the same agent does both actions.
segments.s075-g39: برآید is a classical present-subjunctive/indicative verb “arises/appears” placed before the subject (inverted, poetic word order). یکی باد is an indefinite subject “a certain/a single wind,” and با زلزله (“with an earthquake”) indicates accompaniment.
segments.s075-g40: ز is the poetic preposition “from/out of”; گیتی is the noun “the world/earth.” Again برآید predicates, and the coordinated خروش و خله gives two outcomes “uproar (khurosh) and tumult/commotion (kholeh)” rising from the world. The line uses parallel syntax to magnify the disturbance.
segments.s075-g73: کشیدند is a past 3rd-person plural verb (they did X); here it means 'continued/kept on' (an extended action). می‌تا is a classical connective meaning 'until' and introduces the limit clause. جهان تیره گشت: جهان (subject) + تیره (adjective 'dark') + گشت (past of گشت 'became') — adjective follows noun and گشت marks the resulting change of state.
segments.s075-g74: سرمیگساران is a nominative/nominal phrase 'the chief revelers' (compound noun). ز is the poetic/old preposition 'from' or 'by' indicating cause/agent. می‌خیره گشت is a compound where می‌خیره (an intransitive verb/adjective-like word meaning 'become transfixed/astounded') takes گشت to form a past-state 'became transfixed'; the clause says they were rendered transfixed by wine.
segments.s075-g75: خروشیدن is used as a verbal noun/verb 'to cry out, to roar' describing the sound made by the men. مرد (here plural in sense) is the actor 'men' or 'people'; بالای گاه is a prepositional/location phrase 'on the upper place/platform' (بالای = atop/above, گاه here denotes the place/spot — an older/poetic usage). The phrase describes who is crying out and from where.
segments.s075-g76: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one' modifying برآمد, which is a past tense verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز marks the source 'from' and درگاه شاه is a genitive construction 'the king's court/threshold' (درگاه = royal court/presence, شاه = king). Word order: adverb → verb → source phrase.
segments.s075-g77: برفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they went/departed'. گردان here means 'troop/group' (subject) and the following همه شاد و مست is a predicative description 'all merry and intoxicated' with adjectives following the noun; و simply joins the two adjectives.
segments.s075-g78: گرفته is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'having taken/holding' (literally 'taken'). The phrase یکی دست دیگر به دست is an idiomatic construction: 'one (person) [takes] another's hand' — یکی = one, دست = hand, دیگر = other/another, به دست = into/into the hand (i.e., hand-in-hand). The verb is implicit in the participle گرفته.
segments.s075-g79: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. برزد is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'struck/shot forth/appeared' used with زبانه (a metaphorical 'tongue' of light or flame). ز introduces the origin/source and کوه آفتاب literally 'mountain of the sun' or 'mountain where the sun rises' — together the clause describes sunrise imagery.
segments.s075-g80: سر نامدران is a nominal phrase where سر (here 'chief/head') combines with نامدران ('the renowned/famous ones') to mean 'the foremost or notable people'. برآمد is past 'arose' and ز خواب 'from sleep' indicates the source/state they rose from — 'they awoke/rose from sleep.'
segments.s075-g49: وگر is the conditional marker 'if'. آز (object) is the noun 'greed' and is immediately followed by ورزیم, 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive of ورزیدن 'to practice' (we practice/cultivate). The coordinating و links to پیچان شویم; پیچان is an adjective 'crooked, deceitful' and شویم is the 1st‑person plural subjunctive/passive of شدن 'to become' (we become), so the whole segment means 'if we practice greed and become deceitful'.
segments.s075-g50: پدید آید is an impersonal/poetic combination 'will appear/come forth' (پدید + آید). آنگه 'then' introduces the consequence; که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/when'); بیجان is the predicate adjective 'lifeless, dead'; شویم again is 1st‑person plural 'we become'. The clause thus says 'then it will happen that we become lifeless' with the copular idea carried by شویم.
segments.s075-g51: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایوان ما is a genitive construction 'our hall/porch'. سر here means 'top/summit' (سر به کیوان = 'top to Kayvan/heavens'), and برست is a past/poetic verb meaning 'reached/rose' (past tense in older/formal Persian). The phrase literally reads 'if the summit of our iwan reached to Kayvan'.
segments.s075-g52: ازان بهره ما is 'from that share/portion of ours' (ازان = 'from that'). یکی is the numeral 'one' and چادرست is a nominal predicate — the copula 'is' is omitted in poetry, so the sense is 'one (of our shares) is a tent' (i.e. our share amounts to a single tent).
segments.s075-g53: چو here means 'when'. پوشند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of پوشیدن 'to cover' (they/it covers), used impersonally with the following object. بر روی ما is the prepositional phrase 'over our faces' and خون و خاک are the things doing the covering 'blood and dust'. The verb precedes its object in this poetic order.
segments.s075-g54: همه جای is a fixed phrase 'everywhere' ('all place'). بیمست is an archaic contracted predicate equivalent to بیم است 'is fearful/there is fear' (بیم + است), followed by a nominal list تیمار و باک 'care and dread' as additional predicates/qualities. The copula is incorporated in بیمست; the nouns after it enumerate the states present everywhere.
segments.s075-g55: This is a noun phrase listing setting and agent: بیابان 'the desert' coordinated with آن مرد 'that man'. با تیز داس is an instrumental phrase 'with a sharp scythe' (با = 'with', تیز = 'sharp', داس = 'scythe'), describing the man.
segments.s075-g56: کجا here is rhetorical 'where/wherever' or 'how' used to introduce the result clause. خشک و‌تر pairs 'خشک' (dry) with 'وتر' (the moist/wet) using و as the conjunction; زو is an archaic contraction of از او 'from him' (here shown as ز + و in the segmentation). دل اندر هراس is idiomatic 'the heart is in terror' (اندر = 'in', predicate 'هراس' with the possessor دل). The copula is omitted, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s075-g9: This is an inverted, somewhat archaic word order: به سی روز (literally 'in/after thirty days') sets the temporal frame, مه را is the definite object ('the month' / 'the moon' with را marking it), and سرآید شمار is a verb + noun construction where سرآید (subjunctive/present-future sense 'comes to an end') governs شمار ('the count/tally'). Together: 'the month's count comes to an end in thirty days.'
segments.s075-g10: برین سان is an older orthography of بر این سان ('in this manner'); بود is the past copula 'was' giving the past/state sense, and گردش روزگار is a nominal phrase 'the turning/cycle of the times' (گردش = rotation/circuit, روزگار = time/fate). Literally: 'Thus was the turning of the world.'
segments.s075-g11: کنون 'now' opens the clause. آنکه گفتی is a nominalized relative phrase 'that which you said' (آنکه = 'he/that which'), ز کار 'about/from the affair' gives the topic, and دو اسپ 'two steeds' names the subject-matter; the word order places the relative phrase before the topic for emphasis.
segments.s075-g12: فروزان ('radiant') qualifies the two steeds; به کردار is a prepositional phrase 'in the manner/guise of' (به + noun), and آذرگشسپ is the proper name being referred to. Grammatically this attaches as a descriptive phrase to the previous clause: 'the two radiant steeds, in the manner of Azar‑Gushasp.'
segments.s075-g13: This clause lists two qualities: سپید و سیاهست is a nominal-verb phrase 'bright/white and dark are' (سیاهست = 'is dark'); هر دو زمان 'both times' is the subject ('هر دو' = 'both / each two'). The adjective-noun order is typical, and the copula is folded into the adjective 'سیاهست.'
segments.s075-g14: پس 'then' links the sequence; یکدگر 'one another' marks mutual action; تیز is an adverbial adjective 'swift(ly)'; هر دو 'both' repeats the idea of two subjects, and دوان is a verbal/participial form meaning 'running/galloping.' The phrase means 'then both ran swiftly at one another.'
segments.s075-g15: شب و روز 'night and day' gives the continuous temporal frame. باشد here is a subjunctive/modal copula meaning roughly 'it is (so) that' and introduces the subordinate clause introduced by که, and می‌بگذرد is the habitual/present progressive 'it passes.' Together: 'Night and day it is that (time) passes.'
segments.s075-g16: دم چرخ is a genitive phrase 'the moment/breath of the wheel' where چرخ (wheel/sky/fate) here means the wheel of fate; بر ما 'upon us' marks the target, همی is an emphatic/progressive particle 'continually/indeed', and بشمرد is a present/future-fused verb 'it counts/reckons.' The clause reads: 'the wheel counts each moment upon us.'
segments.s075-g1: 'زمانی' is an adverbial temporal phrase 'at one time/once'. 'پر اندیشه شد' is a predicative construction: 'پر' (full of) + 'اندیشه' (thought) + past tense 'شد' (became), so literally 'became full of thought'. The proper name 'زال' follows the verb in this poetic word order and is the subject. 'زر' is the object/nucleus of the thought (i.e. 'about gold').
segments.s075-g2: 'برآورد یال' is a verbal phrase where 'برآورد' is a past verb meaning 'lifted/raised' (poetic/archaic usage) and 'یال' is the object 'his mane'; the subject (Zal) is understood from the previous clause. The conjunction 'و' links the two actions, and 'بگسترد بر' is another past verb with the repetitive/preterite prefix 'بـ-' meaning 'spread' with the preposition 'بر' ('over/on'), so together 'and spread (it) over'.
segments.s075-g3: 'وزان پس' is a compound adverbial 'and/from that time, then' marking sequence. 'به پاسخ' is a prepositional phrase 'in answer/to answer'. 'زبان برگشاد' is a verb phrase 'he opened his tongue' (برگشاد is a past tense verb 'opened'), where 'زبان' ('tongue' = 'speech') is the direct object and marks that he speaks.
segments.s075-g4: 'همه پرسش موبدان' is a genitive/possessive grouping 'all the priests' questions' (پرسش modified by the following noun موبدان). 'کرد یاد' is the light-verb construction used for 'یاد کردن' = 'to remember/recall', with کرد in the past tense, so the whole means 'he recalled/remembered'.
segments.s075-g5: 'نخست' is an adverb 'first'. 'از' here is partitive 'of/from among', and 'ده و دو' is the poetic numeral 'ten and two' = twelve. The noun phrase 'درخت بلند' follows with the adjective after the noun in typical Persian order: 'tall trees'.
segments.s075-g6: The relative/subordinating 'که' introduces a clause describing the trees. 'هر یک' = 'each one'. 'همی' is the narrative/imperfective marker indicating habitual or iterative action ('would/used to'). 'شاخ' here means 'branch', and 'برکشند' is a plural verb 'they raise/lift' (the plural referring back to the plural concept 'trees' or 'each one' in context), so the clause reads 'each one would raise thirty branches' (شاخ سی is implied in the line).
segments.s075-g7: 'به سالی' is an adverbial phrase 'in a year' (literally 'to a year' = 'per year'). 'ده و دو' is the numeral 'ten and two' = twelve. 'بود' is the past copula 'there was/were'. 'ماه نو' is 'new moon' (ماه = moon/month, modified by نو = new).
segments.s075-g8: 'چو' introduces a temporal/comparative clause 'when/as'. 'شاه نو' is 'the new king' (شاه = king, نو = new). The sequence 'آیین ابر گاه نو' strings nouns and an adjective: آیین (ritual/ceremony) and ابرگاه (cloud‑time/season) with نو ('new') qualifying the phrase; syntactically this is a compressed poetic order saying that with a new king the ritual/season is also new. 'چو' + clause marks the condition/time for that state.
segments.s075-g121: همه is the subject meaning “everyone.” برکشیدند is past, 3rd person plural, ‘they raised/drew’ (here: drew weapons). گردان سلیح is a poetic noun phrase modifying what was drawn: گردان (here adjectival/participial, ‘whirling’/‘turning’) qualifies سلیح ‘weapons,’ so together ‘whirling weapons’ or ‘a whirling mass of arms.’ Word order is slightly marked/poetic (adjective before noun).
segments.s075-g122: بدل here functions adverbially/nominatively as ‘in return’/‘by way of retaliation.’ خشمناک is an adjective ‘angry, furious’ describing them. و simply connects clauses. زبان پر مزیح is a noun phrase: زبان ‘speech/tongue’ + پر ‘full (of)’ (post‑nominal) + مزیح ‘mockery/jest,’ meaning literally ‘a tongue full of mockery’ (i.e., they taunted).
segments.s075-g123: به آورد رفتند combines motion and result: رفتند is simple past ‘they went/rode off.’ به آورد here is a light/resultive phrase meaning roughly ‘having brought/put into (a pace or motion)’ or ‘they brought (their horses) to (a gallop)’ — a poetic causative/resultive use of آورد. پیچان عنان is a participial phrase ‘twisting the reins,’ where پیچان (present participle/adjectival) modifies عنان (‘the reins’) and describes the manner of their going.
segments.s075-g124: This is a coordinated instrumental/nominal phrase listing arms. The first item (ابا نیزه) is unclear in the lemma list and likely functions as ‘with spears’ (با/ابا + نیزه) — here treated as a noun phrase meaning ‘spears.’ و is the conjunction. آب‌داده سنان is a compound: آب‌داده (past participle ‘watered/moistened’) modifies سنان ‘spear‑points/spears,’ so ‘spear‑points moistened with water’ (a conventional image for tempered or blooded spears).
segments.s075-g125: چنان شد که is a fixed narrative formula, ‘so it happened that.’ مرد is the subject ‘a man,’ اندر آمد is the past intransitive ‘entered/ came in’ (اندر = ‘in/among’), and به مرد is a directional/target phrase ‘toward the man’ or ‘to the man’ — literally: ‘a man came in among (them) toward a man.’ The clause is somewhat telegraphic/poetic in order but obeys classical Persian clause structure (چنان شد که + clause).
segments.s075-g126: برانگیخت is a past transitive ‘(he) roused’ or ‘set in motion.’ In this line the verb precedes the proper name (poetic inversion): برانگیخت زال اسپ = ‘Zāl roused (his) steed’ (زال is subject, اسپ object). و links the next action; برخاست is intransitive past ‘(he/it) rose/sprung up,’ and گرد here is an adverb ‘around/about,’ giving the sense ‘sprang up, turning about.’
segments.s075-g127: نگه کرد is a compound verb meaning ‘he kept watch’ or ‘he looked’ (نگه + کرد). تا introduces a subordinate purpose/extent clause ‘to/so that’ and here heads the indirect question کیست ‘who is…’. زیشان is a genitive-like phrase ‘of them/from them’ (زی + شان), and سوار is the predicate noun ‘rider/horseman.’ Together تا کیست زیشان سوار = ‘to see who among them is the rider.’
segments.s075-g128: This is a string of epithets describing that rider. عنان پیچ is a compound where عنان ‘reins’ + پیچ ‘twisting’ (here adjectival/participial) gives ‘reins‑twisting’ (i.e., twisting the reins). و links the next adjectives: گردنکش ‘headstrong/rebellious’ and نامدار ‘renowned/famed.’ The epithets are in apposition to سوار in the previous segment.
segments.s075-g105: بزد is the simple past, 3rd person singular of زدن (‘to strike/hit’); the subject is omitted and understood from context. بر میان دراخت سهی is a prepositional phrase following the verb (typical older/Persian word order allows verb before its complement here): بر + میان = 'upon/in the middle of', درخت 'tree' and سهی an adjectival epithet 'dark/black' modifying درخت.
segments.s075-g106: گذاره شد is a passive/periphrastic expression: گذاره is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'to be placed/come to rest' and شد is the auxiliary 'became/was', so together 'was placed' or 'was lodged'. آن تیر شاهنشهی is the noun phrase ('that imperial arrow') serving as the subject of the passive clause and comes after the verb for emphasis (poetic inversion).
segments.s075-g107: هم is an emphatic/adverbial 'also/even' placed before the locative phrase. اندر is the archaic preposition 'in/into'. تگ here describes the narrow/side part of the horse (rendered 'flank'); syntactically تگ اسپ is a noun phrase 'the (narrow) side of the steed'. یک چوبه تیر is a quantified noun phrase 'one shaft (stick) of an arrow' that functions as the object and appears before the verb (object-before-verb order is common in classical Persian poetry).
segments.s075-g108: بینداخت is past tense 'he threw/cast' (بیـ prefix + انداخت). و simply links clauses ('and'). بگذاشت is past 'he put/left/placed' (بـ- prefix + گذاشت); here it continues the action begun by بینداخت. چون + نره شیر is a simile 'like a male lion' (چون = 'like/as', نره 'male', شیر 'lion'), describing the manner of the action. The verbs are finite past forms describing successive actions.
segments.s075-g109: سپر برگرفتند is a past plural clause 'they took up shields' (سپر object + برگرفتند 'they lifted/took up'). ژوپین‌وران follows as the subject/nominative plural 'the juppin‑wearers' (armor‑wearers); the order is inverted for poetic effect but the verb برگرفتند agrees in plural with ژوپین‌وران.
segments.s075-g110: بگشتند is past plural 'they returned/turned (back)'. با is the preposition 'with' marking accompaniment or instrument. خشتهای is the plural construct 'mud‑bricks' (خشت + ـهای plural suffix) and گران is an adjective 'heavy/weighty' modifying خشتهای. The whole phrase states how they moved away (with heavy bricks).
segments.s075-g111: سپر خواست is a transitive past clause where سپر 'shield' is the object and خواست is the 3rd person singular past 'he asked/wanted'. از marks the source or from whom the request is made. ریدک ترک is an epithet + ethnic designation 'the (insignificant/little) Turk Ridak' (here ریدک functions as a descriptive name/epithet modifying ترک). زال at the end is the subject 'Zal' — placing the subject after the verb and prepositional phrase is characteristic of epic/poetic inversion.
segments.s075-g112: برانگیخت is past 'he roused/raised' (here transitive in the sense 'he put the horse in motion / reared it up'). اسپ is the direct object 'horse/steed'. و links to the next action. برآورد + یال: برآورد here is a classical/periphrastic past meaning 'he brought up/raised' (i.e., tossed up), and یال 'mane' is its object — together 'he raised/brandished the mane' (a short, vivid action on the horse).
segments.s075-g65: This clause is a compact, poetic statement: ازین در (‘from this door/gate’) is a prepositional phrase (از + این, here written ازین) giving the origin; درآید is a 3rd‑person singular verb (present/past‑narrative use) meaning “comes in/enters” used generically; بدان is a direction “to that” (here written as ب + دان in the segmentation) and بگذرد is a 3rd‑person verb “passes.” Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect — literal sequence: “from this door he comes, to that he passes.” Note: the segment’s internal split (ب + دان) is orthographic/poetic; دان in this context functions as part of the demonstrative direction rather than the lexical verb “to know.”
segments.s075-g66: زمانه here addresses Time/Fate (vocative/subject). برو is an imperative/hortative sense “go” or “pass on.” دم is used as a brief span of time (= “moment” / “breath”), همی marks continuing or emphatic aspect (“still / indeed”), and بشمرد is a 3rd‑person present imperfective “he/it counts” or “reckons.” Together: “O Time, go on; (you) even count a single moment.”
segments.s075-g67: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” زال is the subject (the named speaker). این سخنها is the direct object (“these words/remarks”), and بکرد is the past tense 3rd‑person verb “he made / he did.” آشکار is a predicative adjective/adverb “clear” / “plain” that completes the verb: “made (them) clear.” Word order (subject then object then verb + predicate) reflects older/poetic Persian.
segments.s075-g68: ازو is a prepositional/causal marker “from him / because of him.” شادمان is an adjective “glad / joyful.” شد is the past‑tense copulative “became.” دل شهریار is the affected noun phrase “the heart of the king / the king’s heart.” Structure: ‘Because of him the king’s heart became joyful.’
segments.s075-g69: به شادی is a circumstantial phrase “for joy / in celebration.” یکی انجمن is “an assembly / a certain gathering.” برشگفت is a past action meaning roughly “rose up / sprang into being” (poetic past form). The whole segment: “An assembly arose for the celebration.”
segments.s075-g70: شهنشاه گیتی here is a title “the king (or sovereign) of the world.” زهازه is a prepositional phrase “from it / from that” (here meaning ‘from the assembly/occasion’), and گرفت is the past‑tense verb “took / seized.” Together the clause means the sovereign took (pleasure/delight) from it — i.e. he enjoyed the occasion. The exact object (‘delight’) is implicit in the Persian ellipsis.
segments.s075-g71: یکی is “a certain / one,” جشنگاهی is a compound noun “place or occasion of festivity,” بیاراست is a past verb “he adorned / prepared,” and شاه is the subject “the king.” Word order is object‑verb‑subject (poetic): “A certain festival-place the king prepared.”
segments.s075-g72: چنان چون = “such as / like.” شب چارده is the phrase “the fourteenth night” (lunar fourteenth, i.e. nearly full moon), چرخ ماه is a poetic expression for the moon’s disk/face (literally “wheel of the moon”). The whole simile: “like the moon on the fourteenth night.”
segments.s075-g113: کمان را بینداخت و ژوپین گرفت is two coordinated past actions. کمان را places کمان (bow) as a definite direct object with the object marker را after it. بینداخت is the simple past (3rd‑person singular) of انداختن used here in the sense “threw/cast (down).” The conjunction و links the next clause: ژوپین گرفت has ژوپین as its object (no را; direct object can be bare in classical style) and گرفت is the simple past (3rd‑person singular) of گرفتن meaning “took/seized” (here: took up or put on a gambeson).
segments.s075-g114: به ژوپین شکار نوآیین گرفت continues the action and gives the manner/garment put on. به here marks direction/onto (“on/over” the gambeson). ژوپین is repeated (the padded coat). شکار نوآیین is a noun phrase in which نوآیین (new, novel) qualifies شکار (here: a hunting garment/kit — “hunting” as the relevant sense), and گرفت is again past “took/put on.” The phrase thus reads roughly “(he) put on a new‑fashioned hunting coat over (his) gambeson.”
segments.s075-g115: بزد خشت بر سه سپر گیل‌وار begins with the past verb بزد (3rd‑person singular of زدن, “he struck/smote”). خشت (a lump/brick of clay) is the direct object following the verb. بر introduces the target of the blow (“against/upon”) and سه سپر is the counted object (“three shields”); گیل‌وار is a postpositive adjective meaning “clay‑like” (lit. ‘Gilan‑like’ but here adjectival = ‘clayey’), modifying سپر.
segments.s075-g116: گشاده به دیگر سو افگند خوار is a clause describing the manner and direction of the throw. گشاده is an adverbial/qualifying word meaning “wide/openly” (indicating how far or how openly he threw). به دیگر سو is a directional phrase “to the other side” (به = toward, دیگر = other, سو = side/toward). افگند is the past of افکندن (“he threw/cast”). خوار is placed after the verb and qualifies the action as done with contempt/derision (“in scorn/despisingly”).
segments.s075-g117: به گردنکشان گفت شاه جهان is an inverted word order common in epic style: the preposition به marks the indirect object “to the گردنکشان” (literally “neck‑raisers,” i.e. rebels/proud ones). گفت is the past verb “said” and شاه جهان (literally “king of the world”) is the speaker as a title placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s075-g118: که با او که جوید نبرد از مهان contains two interrogative/relative که clauses (= “who”/“who is it that…”). The first, که با او, asks “who is with him?” (با = with, او = he). The second, که جوید نبرد از مهان, is another question: جوید is the present/stative form ‘seeks/desires’ (3rd‑person), نبرد is “battle,” and از مهان means “from/among the guests” (از here marking source/among). Together: “Who is with him? Who among the guests seeks battle?”
segments.s075-g119: یکی برگراییدش اندر نبرد opens with یکی “one/someone” acting as an indefinite imperative/command target. برگراییدش is a causative/imperative‑coloured past form meaning “cause him to return/restore him” (the suffix ‑ش = him). اندر = in/into, and نبرد = battle; the clause is an order to restore/return that man into the fight.
segments.s075-g120: که از تیر و ژوپین برآورد گرد is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by که = “for/that/who.” از marks source/means (“from/of”), تیر and ژوپین are the things named (arrows and the gambeson). برآورد here is an older/periphrastic perfective/past participial sense of برآوردن (“brought forth/removed/put forth” in classical usages) and گرد is an adverbial particle that with verbs can indicate motion/turning or completion (‘about/away’). Together the clause explains why he should be brought back into battle—literally, “for he came out from (despite) arrows and gambeson” (i.e. he has risen/acted regardless of arrows and coat).
segments.s075-g97: کمانها گرفتند is a simple past (3rd person plural) clause: گرفتند (they took/seized) with کمانها (bows) as its object; word order is verb after object here because of the poetic line flow. The conjunction و links that verb phrase to the following noun phrase تیر خدنگ, which continues the action (i.e. they took bows and [took] dagger-like arrows).
segments.s075-g98: نشانه نهادند is a compound verb (نهادند, they placed/set) with نشانه functioning here as 'target/aim' (classical Persian uses نشانه نهادن = to take aim). چون روز جنگ is a simile meaning 'as on the day of battle' (چون = like/as; روز = day; جنگ = battle). The verb نهادند is past (3rd pl.) and ties back to the agents of گرفتند in the previous segment.
segments.s075-g99: بپیچید is a past (3rd person plural) or narrative-present classical form meaning 'they turned' or 'they veered'; here it governs the action of the riders. هر یک ('each one') is the subject, and به چیزی عنان ('toward some thing, the reins') indicates the direction/target of the turning: عنان (reins) is the thing being turned 'toward' something using the preposition به.
segments.s075-g100: This is a list introduced by repeated به (to/toward), a common poetic device to give rhythm and to mark each item individually: 'toward a mace and toward a sword and toward an arrow and spear.' Each weapon follows its own به, so the prepositional relation (direction/choice of weapon) is explicit for every item.
segments.s075-g101: درختی is an indefinite/poetic way to say 'a tree' (the -ی can soften/indefinitize). The adjectives/qualifiers that follow (here written as گشن بد) are irregular/archaic in form and in this line describe the tree; به میدان شاه is a locative prepositional phrase ('in/at the king's field') where میدان (field/arena) takes the genitive modifier شاه ('of the king').
segments.s075-g102: گذشته برو here functions as a periphrastic perfective phrase meaning 'having passed' or 'went by'; both words together report the lapse of time. سال بسیار و ماه is the time expression 'many years and months' (بسیار modifying سال, و linking ماه). In classical Persian such temporal phrases commonly follow the verb cluster.
segments.s075-g103: کمان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the bow' as definite/direct object; بمالید is the past verb 'he rubbed' (here preceding the subject because of poetic inversion). دستان سام ('the hands of Sam') is the agent phrase that follows the verb in this poetic word order, indicating that Sam rubbed the bow with his hands.
segments.s075-g104: برانگیخت (past) means 'he roused' and takes اسپ ('horse') as its object: 'he roused his steed.' The conjunction و links this clause to برآورد نام: برآورد is another past verb meaning 'brought forth/uttered' here, and نام is the direct object ('a name' or 'a cry/word' in context). The sequence describes consecutive actions by the same subject (Sam).
segments.s075-g137: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' and governs شیران ('lions' or metaphorically 'brave men'). نزاید is the third-person present (negated with ن-) from the root زادیدن, here meaning 'is born' / 'arises'. چنين ("such") and نیز ("also/too") modify گرد, which in this passage functions metaphorically as 'a throng' or 'dust/host' — the noun comes after the verb phrase in this compressed, poetic word order.
segments.s075-g138: چه here is exclamatory/interrogative ('how/what'), introducing the rhetorical question. گرد repeats the same noun 'throng/dust', followed by the prepositional phrase از نهنگانش ('from/of his nahanegans' — i.e. giants). باید شمرد is a modal construction: باید ('it is necessary' / 'must') + شمرد (subjunctive/infinitival form 'to count/reckon'), so together 'must be counted' or 'how many must be reckoned'.
segments.s075-g139: This cluster is a sequence of epithets and noun: خنک (an epithet, here rendered 'fresh/bright' or laudatory) precedes the proper name سام. یل کش is a compound: یل ('hero') + کش (agentive element '‑slayer'), so 'hero‑slayer' or 'killer of heroes.' چنين یادگار ('such a memorial') is the noun phrase being described and will be the subject of the next verbal phrase.
segments.s075-g140: بماند is an optative/third‑person jussive 'may it remain' / 'let it remain.' به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (به + گیتی). دلیر و سوار are the beneficiaries/recipients in an ablative/benefactive sense — 'for the brave and the riders/horsemen' — placed after the locative for rhythm; the verb is understood to govern the whole phrase.
segments.s075-g141: آفرین کرد is the verbal phrase 'he praised/applauded' (آفرین as the noun/exclamation 'bravo' + کردن 'to do' → 'to applaud'). برو here appears as an interjection preceding the exclamation (literally 'go' in its basic sense but functioning idiomatically as an exclamatory particle in the line). شاه بزرگ is the appositive subject 'the great king' performing the action.
segments.s075-g142: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the very', tying the following epithets back to the king. نامور ('renowned') and مهتران سترگ form stacked epithets in apposition — 'famed one, foremost of the mighty/chief ones.' This is a compact, honorific string describing the king; the exact case endings are omitted in epic diction but the nouns are in apposition to شاه.
segments.s075-g143: بزرگان 'the great ones' is the plural subject. سوی کاخ شاه is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward the king's palace' (سوی = toward; کاخ شاه = 'palace of the king' in genitive apposition). آمدند is the simple past/plural verb 'they came.' Word order places the direction before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s075-g144: کمر بسته is a participial phrase 'with the waist bound' (i.e., 'girt, prepared for action'); و links this to the next descriptive phrase. با کلاه آمدند 'they came with hats/helmets' repeats آمدند; such paired participial phrases are common in epic to describe appearance and readiness. بسته is adjectival 'bound/tied' modifying کمر.
segments.s075-g129: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here marking origin or emergence). گرد is used as 'around/from the crowd' (poetic noun) and اندر آمد is a past tense clause 'came in/entered'; بسان + noun is a simile 'like a ...' placed after the verb phrase for emphasis (bâsân نهنگ = 'like a whale').
segments.s075-g130: گرفتش is گرفت (past 'seized') plus the enclitic ـش = 'him/it' (object clitic). کمربند او را is the explicit object 'his belt' with را as the definite/object marker; the object follows the verb here with the clitic duplicating it. به + چنگ is instrumental: 'with/by (his) claw' — چنگ here means 'claw, talon', not 'harp'.
segments.s075-g131: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' modifying the ensuing action. خوارش is خوار + ـش: here the adjective/verb-stem 'خوار' ('low/despised') with the clitic ـش functioning as the object 'him' — idiomatically 'he brought him low' or 'he humbled/overthrew him'. از پشت زین = 'from off the saddle' (از + locative پشت + زین 'saddle'). برگرفت is past 'took up/removed' (here 'took down/removed [from the saddle]').
segments.s075-g132: که introduces a result/explanatory clause 'so that / such that'. شاه و سپه is the compound subject 'the king and the host/army'. ماند is past 'remained/was left', and اندر شگفت is an archaic prepositional phrase 'in wonder/astonishment' (اندر = in).
segments.s075-g133: به آواز is an adverbial phrase 'with a voice / aloud' (به used adverbially). گفتند is the past plural 'they said/cried out'. گردنکشان is the subject 'the proud ones' (literally 'neck-raisers' or 'neck-breakers'), placed after the verb (inverted order typical in epic narration).
segments.s075-g134: که introduces reported content: a prohibitive/result clause. ساختِ جمله is inverted: مردم نبیند کسی زین نشان reads literally 'that among the people no one should see this sign.' نبیند is the classical negative present/subjunctive (or jussive) meaning roughly 'let not ... see' / 'should not see' (used here as a prohibition or strong wish).
segments.s075-g135: هر آن کس که ... is the standard relative construction 'whoever/whosoever'. با او بجوید نبرد places بجوید (present/subjunctive or habitual 'seeks/desires') with نبرد 'battle' as its object: 'whoever seeks battle with him'. The verb بجوید is a present-subjunctive/habitual form common in classical Persian for general statements.
segments.s075-g136: کند is the main verb 'does/will do' used here as a future/optative 'shall give/make'. جامه مادر is the object 'the mother's garment/robe' (a prize). برو لاژورد is a binomial describing the robe's material/color: لاژورد = 'lapis-lazuli' (the precious blue), and برو here is an older/poetic element attached to the color/metal/stone phrase to qualify it (together meaning 'a robe of lapis-lazuli').
segments.s075-g89: بدو here is the dative/indirect-object clitic meaning 'to him' (not the imperative 'run'); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (an ordinary narrative past); شاه‌ای is a vocative 'O king' (شاه with the vocative -ای); جوانمرد is an epithet 'brave/chivalrous man'. The sequence is an addressed quotation introduction: 'He said to him, “O king, brave man ...”'.
segments.s075-g90: یک modifies the (understood) object 'one (spear)'; امروز 'today' gives the time. نیزت combines نیز 'also' with the enclitic -ت (to you / your) making it 'also for you' or 'you also'; بباید is the modal 'must/ought' (present necessity), and سپرد is the lexical verb 'to hand over/entrust' (here as the complement of باید). The clause expresses deontic obligation: 'you must hand over one today as well.'
segments.s075-g91: ترا here is the archaic/poetic combination of the 2nd-person clitic ت plus the object marker را, functioning as 'for you' or 'to you' (indirect object). بویه دخت مهراب is a noun phrase 'Buyeh, daughter of Mehrab' (proper-name + daughter in apposition). خاست is the past verb 'arose/appeared' (classical past form). The clause states that this woman came/appeared for the addressee.
segments.s075-g92: دلت is دل 'heart' with the 2nd-person enclitic -ت ('your heart'). راهش is راه 'way/path' plus the 3rd-person enclitic -ش ('its/his/her way') and functions as the noun being questioned. سام زابل is a proper-name phrase 'Sam of Zabul' in apposition, and کجاست is the interrogative copula 'where is'. The word order places the possessed noun before the predicate: 'your heart—where is its way to Sam of Zabul?'
segments.s075-g93: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / that ...'), licensing the subordinate verb in the following clause. سنج و هندی درای is a coordination: سنج (an instrument word; here an instrument/name) and هندی 'Indian' together modified by درای (an adjective/participial form meaning 'possessing/with wealth' or a descriptive word). The phrase names what was ordered to be brought.
segments.s075-g94: به میدان is the locative phrase 'into/onto the field' (به + میدان). گذارند is a present/jussive plural form 'they place/put/bring' (subordinated to تا, so translated as 'be brought/let them place'). با is the instrumental 'with'; کره نای is 'a small/young ney (reed‑flute)' where کره marks smallness/young and نای is the instrument. The clause describes how/with what they should be placed in the field.
segments.s075-g95: This segment lists the weapons held: نیزه 'spear', گرز 'mace', تیر 'arrow', کمان 'bow', coordinated with و 'and'. The initial token ابا is archaic/poetic and here functions as a prepositional/participial element meaning roughly 'with' or 'bearing' (the lemma senses given do not match this usage), introducing the instrumental list.
segments.s075-g96: برفتند is the past plural verb 'they went/advanced/departed'. گردان is the subject 'troop/unit' and follows the verb here (a common poetic/variable word order). همه 'all' quantifies شادمان 'joyful/rejoicing' (an adjective/noun), so 'the whole troop, all joyful'. The clause is a straightforward past narrative statement.
segments.s075-g17: سدیگر functions as a topicalizer meaning “another (thing)” or “the other matter.” The first که introduces reported speech (‘that’), and گفتی is the 2nd-person past ‘you said’ (here introducing what was said). The second که begins the subordinate content clause; آن سی سوار is a demonstrative + numeral + noun sequence ‘those thirty horsemen’ with the demonstrative آن before the noun.
segments.s075-g18: کجا is the interrogative ‘where’. برگذشتند is the past 3rd-plural of گذشتن with the verbal prefix بر- giving the sense ‘passed by/past’; the verb thus asks about their motion. بر + شهریار forms a prepositional phrase specifying motion with respect to the king (‘past/by the king’), so the clause means ‘where did they pass by the king?’
segments.s075-g19: ازان سی سواران is a partitive/genitive phrase ‘of those thirty horsemen’ (ازان = ‘of those’). یکی کم شود is a predicate with یکی as the indefinite subject ‘one’ and کم شدن (‘to be less/be lacking’) expressed with the adjective کم plus شود ‘becomes/is’ (شدن + شود), giving the sense ‘one would be missing’ or ‘one turns out to be lacking’. The verb شود is used descriptively/habitually.
segments.s075-g20: به گاه شمردن is a temporal prepositional phrase ‘at the time of counting’ (به + گاه + the infinitive/masdar شمردن). همان سی بود is a nominal clause with همان سی ‘that same thirty’ as predicate and بود as the past copula ‘was’ — standard Persian order where the predicative phrase precedes the copula.
segments.s075-g21: نگفتی is the past 2nd-person form with the negative prefix ن- (‘you did not say’ / in context idiomatically ‘you spoke only’); here it frames a restriction. سخن is the object ‘speech/words’, and جز ز + نقصان ماه is a prepositional/exceptive phrase ‘except from the moon’s decrease’ (جز followed by poetic ز for ‘of/from’). Together: ‘you said nothing except about the moon’s waning.’
segments.s075-g22: که introduces the explanatory subordinate clause ‘that…’. یک شب کم آید is a small clause where یک شب (‘one night’) is the theme and کم آید (کم + آید) means ‘is (or becomes) short/missing’; آید is the present/habitual form ‘comes/happens’. همی (an intensifier/continuous marker) + گاه‌گاه ‘occasionally’ modifies the clause: ‘that one night is lacking now and then.’
segments.s075-g23: کنون ‘now’ sets the temporal frame. از نیام is a poetic ablative ‘from the scabbard/sheath’ (نیام = scabbard), and این سخن is the direct object ‘this speech/tale’. برکشیم is 1st-person-plural hortative/subjunctive ‘let us draw up/raise/draw forth’ (برکشیدن + -یم used to invite action), so the whole segment means ‘now let us draw this tale from the scabbard.’
segments.s075-g24: دو بن سرو is a noun phrase ‘two trunks/bases of cypress’ (بن = base/root/trunk, سرو = cypress). کان functions here as a relative/adverbial ‘from/where’ (classical/poetic usage), introducing the following clause. مرغ دارد is ‘a bird has’ or more idiomatically ‘there is a bird’ (i.e., a place for a bird), and نشیم is a hortative ‘let us sit/let us alight’ (1st-plural subjunctive). The line pictures two cypress-trunks on which (or where) a bird may alight.
segments.s075-g57: تر و خشک یکسان همی بدرود is an adjectival/nominal phrase describing what the subject does: 'تر' (green/wet) and 'خشک' (dry) joined by 'و' form a coordinated object; 'یکسان' qualifies them as equal; 'همی' is the archaic present-tense continuous marker ('still/always' or habitual); and 'بدرود' is the main predicate in an archaic/poetic form meaning something like 'cuts/takes away' (the verb form is elliptical/poetic here). Word order is noun(s) → adverb/adjective → verb (classical inversion/ellipsis is common).
segments.s075-g58: وگر لابه سازی سخن نشنود is a conditional/adversative clause. 'وگر' = 'but if'; 'لابه سازی' is a compound noun formed from 'لابه' + 'سازی' and here means 'making lamentation/wailing' (poetic, expressed as a verbal-noun); 'سخن' = 'words/speech'; and 'نشنود' is the negative present form 'does not hear' (classical third-person). The clause says that even if lamentation is made, the subject does not hear the speech.
segments.s075-g59: دروگر زمانست و ما چون گیا is an equative/parallel clause: 'دروگر' (the reaper) is equated to 'زمان' by 'زمانست' (classical copula 'is time'); 'و' links the next clause 'ما چون گیا' where 'ما' is the subject 'we' and 'چون' functions as a preposition 'like', followed by 'گیا' (plants/vegetation). The clause sets up the metaphor: Time = reaper; we = plants.
segments.s075-g60: همانش نبیره همانش نیا is a terse, elliptical poetic statement. Each 'همانش' ('the same/that very') topicalizes the following noun: 'نبیره' = 'offspring/descendant' and 'نیا' = 'ancestor'; the verb ('is' / 'exists' / 'has') is omitted in classical poetry, so the line reads as 'the same — no offspring; the same — no ancestor' (i.e., no one is offspring of another nor ancestor of another, or the same condition applies to all). The repetition creates parallelism.
segments.s075-g61: به پیر و جوان یک به یک ننگرد: 'به' introduces the objects 'پیر و جوان' (the old and the young). The adverbial phrase 'یک به یک' means 'one by one' or 'individually'. 'ننگرد' (classical present negative of نگر کردن/نگر) means 'does not look upon/does not regard'. Together it says the subject does not examine old and young individually.
segments.s075-g62: شکاری که پیش آیدش بشکرد is a relative clause modifying 'شکاری' (quarry/prey/hunting matter). 'که' = 'which/that'; 'پیش آیدش' = 'comes before him' (آید + enclitic -ش for 'to him'); and 'بشکرد' is an archaic/poetic verb meaning roughly 'strikes/breaks/overcomes' (here 'smite' or 'seize'). The whole clause states that any prey that comes before him he will deal with decisively.
segments.s075-g63: جهان را چنینست ساز و نهاد: 'جهان را' uses the object/ topical marker 'را' with 'جهان' to front the topic; 'چنینست' is the copular predicate 'is thus'; 'ساز و نهاد' are two coordinated nouns meaning 'making/structure' and 'disposition/foundation' describing the world's constitution. The phrase says 'the world's making and order are thus.'
segments.s075-g64: که جز مرگ را کس ز مادر نزاد is a subordinate explanatory clause introduced by 'که' = 'that'. 'جز مرگ' = 'except death'; the word-order is emphatic/poetic: 'مرگ را کس' (death — person) then 'ز مادر نزاد' = 'is born of a mother'. The net meaning (elliptical) is that, from a mother, no one is born except death — i.e., mortality is the inescapable outcome of birth.
segments.s075-g145: Word order here is object–verb–subject for emphasis: 'یکی' (a certain person) is the direct object, 'خلعت آراست' is the verb phrase 'adorned with a robe', and 'شاه جهان' is the subject 'the king of the world'. 'آراست' is a past tense transitive verb (he adorned/dressed).
segments.s075-g146: 'که' here links the previous clause to the following result (roughly 'and/so'), 'گشتند' is past plural of 'گشتن' meaning 'became/turned', and the phrase 'ازان خیره یکسر مهان' means 'from that (at that sight) the guests were completely astonished' — 'خیره' = stunned/astonished, 'یکسر' = entirely, 'مهان' = the guests/visitors.
segments.s075-g147: The exclamatory 'چه از' introduces one item in a parallel list ('what of / whether from'). 'تاج پرمایه' is a noun phrase 'a crown full/rich (of gems)' where 'پرمایه' qualifies 'تاج'. 'تخت زر' is 'the golden throne' (noun + attribute 'زر' = gold).
segments.s075-g148: The parallel second part introduced again by 'چه از' lists other regalia: 'یاره' (here part of an enumerated set of valuables), 'طوق' (necklace/collar), and 'زرین کمر' ('golden girdle' — adjective 'زرین' before the noun 'کمر').
segments.s075-g149: 'همان' = 'those/the same' identifies the previously mentioned garments; 'جامه‌های گرانمایه' is a plural noun phrase 'precious/expensive garments' (noun + adjective), and 'نیز' adds 'also/too'.
segments.s075-g150: This is a further list of items: 'پرستنده' (here 'attendants/devoted followers'), 'اسپ' (horse/steed), followed by 'هر گونه چیز' ('every kind of thing' — 'هر' quantifies 'گونه چیز'). The items are coordinated with 'و'.
segments.s075-g151: 'به زال' marks the recipient with the dative 'to Zāl', 'سپهبد' is the subject 'the spahbed', and 'سپرد' is the past tense verb 'entrusted/handed over'. 'آن زمان' is a temporal phrase 'at that time' placed after the verb for stylistic order.
segments.s075-g152: 'همه چیزها' = 'all things/everything'; the prepositional idiom 'از کران تا کران' literally 'from edge to edge' means 'from one end to the other' (i.e., completely, everywhere). Together this specifies what was entrusted.
segments.s075-g81: Poetic word order: the past singular verb بیامد (came) stands first, followed by the participial adjective کمربسته 'girt/girded' describing his condition, then the name زال and its epithet دلیر 'brave'. Together they form 'Zal the brave, girded, came.'
segments.s075-g82: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into/into the presence of' (به = to; پیش = in the presence of), followed by the ruler's title شهنشاه. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and نره شیر is a noun phrase 'male lion' (نره specifying the male). There is no copula — the comparison attaches directly to Zal's coming.
segments.s075-g83: This phrase begins with به دستوری where به is used adverbially 'by' (introducing the means) and دستوری = 'by command'. بازگشتن is the infinitive 'to return', and ز در is the poetic 'from the gate/door' (ز = از). Together: 'to return from the gate by command.'
segments.s075-g84: شدن here is used in a poetic/archaic sense meaning 'to approach/go to' (not a modern reflexive auxiliary); نزد 'in the presence of' introduces the beneficiary/location, followed by the title سالار 'commander/chief' and the following name/epithet and پدر 'father' (appositive: 'Farakh, his father').
segments.s075-g85: به شاه جهان = 'to the King of the World' (به = to, شاه جهان compound title). گفت is the speech verb 'said' (note: the provided sense list did not include the verbal sense). کای is a vocative 'O' (archaic/high style) and نیکخوی is an epithet 'well‑bred / good‑natured', forming the direct address.
segments.s075-g86: This clause has an older word order: مرا = 'to me' (dative; here split in the tokenization), چهر سام = 'the face/appearance of Sam' (چهر head noun followed by the name سام), آمدست is the perfect 'has come', and آرزوی 'as a desire/longing' closes the clause. Literal: 'Sam's face has come to me as a desire.'
segments.s075-g87: ببوسیدم is first‑person past 'I kissed'. This is followed by the demonstrative این 'this' and the noun phrase پایه تخت عاج: پایه 'pedestal/base' governed by تخت 'throne', modified by عاج 'ivory' (i.e. 'the pedestal of this ivory throne').
segments.s075-g88: دل‌م (tokenized here as دل + م) = 'my heart' (م is the possessive suffix '-am'). گشت روشن = 'became bright/clear'. بدین is the prepositional/adverbial 'by/with this' (here written in two tokens), followed by برز 'exaltation/highness' and و تاج 'and crown' — 'my heart became bright with this exaltation and crown.'
